These results show that either a large number of underlying loci of small effect and/or a linear parent-offspring regression generally will recover the simple breeder's equation $$ R=h^{2}S $$ (13.1) plus correction terms that are often small. Taking the expectation over all selected parents, the expected offspring mean after selection is $$ \begin{aligned}E_{s}\big[E(z_{o}\mid z_{fa},z_{mo})\big]&=\mu_{o}+b_{o,fa}E_{s}\big[\left(z_{fa}-\mu_{fa}\right)\big]+b_{o,mo}E_{s}\big[\left(z_{mo}-\mu_{mo}\right)\big]\\&=\mu_{o}+b_{o,fa}S_{fa}+b_{o,mo}S_{mo}\end{aligned} $$ (13.3) where $ S_{fa} $ and $ S_{mo} $ are the directional selection differentials for fathers and mothers.

formulas.label: Formula 13.6b
formulas.context_text: nt is a better measure of the strength of association than a covariance (LW Chapter 3). Substituting $ \bar{\imath}\sigma_z $ for $ S $ gives the selection-intensity version of the breeder's equation $$ R=h^{2}\bar{\imath}\sigma_{z}=\bar{\imath}h\sigma_{A}=\sigma_{A}^{2}\bar{\imath}/\sigma_{z} $$ (13.6b) which follows from $$ h^{2}\sigma_{z}=\frac{\sigma_{A}^{2}}{\sigma_{z}^{2}}\sigma_{z}=\frac{\sigma_{A}}{\sigma_{z}}\sigma_{A}=h\sigma_{A} $$

formulas.label: Formula 13.7a
formulas.context_text: As introduced in Chapter 6, the selection differential can be written as the covariance between relative fitness and trait value $$ S=\sigma(w,z) $$ (13.7a)

formulas.label: Formula 13.8a
formulas.context_text: An important application of the Robertson-Price identity follows if we consider the slope of the least-squares linear regression of relative fitness $ (w) $ on phenotypic value, z $$ w=a+\beta z+e $$ (13.8a)

formulas.label: Formula 13.11e

formulas.context_text: traits included in the analysis, i.e., all traits in the vector z (Chapter 30). Because $ \beta = P^{-1}S $, then $ \mathbf{S} = \mathbf{P}\beta $, resulting in an observed selection differential of $$ S_{i}=P_{ii}\beta_{i}+\sum_{j\neq i}P_{ij}\beta_{j} $$ (13.25c)

formulas.label: Formula 13.26b
formulas.subsection: Accounting for Genetic Correlations: The Lande Equation
formulas.context_text: n (Lande 1979a), $$ \mathbf{R}=\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\beta} $$ (13.26a) which is the multivariate version of Equation 13.8c, $ R = \sigma_A^2 \beta $. Considering two traits, using the Lande equation $$ \mathbf{R}=\begin{pmatrix}R_{1}\\ R_{2}\end{pmatrix}=\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\beta}=\begin{pmatrix}G_{11}&G_{12}\\ G_{12}&G_{22}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\beta_{1}\\ \beta_{2}\end{pmatrix} $$ (13.26b) yields greatly simplified expressions (relative to Equation 13.24c) for the responses $$ R_{1}=G_{11}\beta_{1}+G_{12}\beta_{2} $$ (13.26c) $$ R_{2}=G_{12}\beta_{1}+G_{22}\beta_{2} $$

formulas.label: Formula 13.26c
formulas.subsection: Accounting for Genetic Correlations: The Lande Equation
formulas.context_text: n{pmatrix}G_{11}&G_{12}\\ G_{12}&G_{22}\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}\beta_{1}\\ \beta_{2}\end{pmatrix} $$ (13.26b) yields greatly simplified expressions (relative to Equation 13.24c) for the responses $$ R_{1}=G_{11}\beta_{1}+G_{12}\beta_{2} $$ (13.26c) $$ R_{2}=G_{12}\beta_{1}+G_{22}\beta_{2} $$

formulas.label: Formula 13.26d
formulas.subsection: Accounting for Genetic Correlations: The Lande Equation
formulas.context_text: two traits are genetically uncorrelated, selection on one has no impact on the response of the other, even if they are phenotypically correlated. More generally, with n potentially correlated traits $$ R_{i}=G_{ii}\beta_{i}+\sum_{j\neq i}G_{ij}\beta_{j} $$ (13.26d)

formulas.label: Formula 13.27a
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: nal selection gradient satisfies $ \beta = \partial \ln \overline{W} / \partial \mu $ (Lande 1976; Example A6.3 gives the full multivariate derivation). Hence we can express the breeder's equation as $$ R=\sigma_{A}^{2}\left(\frac{\partial\ln\overline{W}}{\partial\mu}\right) $$ (13.27a)

formulas.label: Formula 13.27b
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: erivative is the gradient of mean fitness with respect to the vector of character means, which is the vector of partials of the log of mean fitness with respect to each trait mean under consideration $$ \beta=\frac{\partial\ln\overline{W}}{\partial\mu} $$ (13.27b) with $ \beta_i = \partial \ln \overline{W} / \partial \mu_i $ (the change in log mean fitness from a change in the mean of trait $ i $). The resulting gradient version of the Lande equation becomes $

formulas.label: Formula 13.27c
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: ) with $ \beta_i = \partial \ln \overline{W} / \partial \mu_i $ (the change in log mean fitness from a change in the mean of trait $ i $). The resulting gradient version of the Lande equation becomes $$ \mathbf{R}=\mathbf{G}\ \frac{\partial\ln\overline{W}}{\partial\mu} $$ (13.27c)

formulas.label: Formula 13.28a
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: quantity, the amount of selection, $ \beta $, on a vector, z, of traits, as follows. Walsh and Blows (2009) showed that the additive variance in relative fitness w accounted for by selection on z is $$ \sigma_{A}(\mathbf{z}^{T},w)\mathbf{G}^{-1}\sigma_{A}(\mathbf{z},w) $$ (13.28a) where the notation $ \sigma_A(x, y) $ denotes the covariance between the breeding values of $ x $ and $ y $. Recalling Robertson's secondary theorem (1968 version; Equation 6.24a), $ \sigma_A(\mathbf

formulas.label: Formula 13.28b
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: bertson's secondary theorem (1968 version; Equation 6.24a), $ \sigma_A(\mathbf{z}, w) = \mathbf{R} = \mathbf{G}\beta $ (assuming the conditions for the multivariate breeder's equation hold), yielding $$ \left(\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\beta}\right)^{T}\mathbf{G}^{-1}\left(\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\beta}\right)=\boldsymbol{\beta}^{T}\mathbf{G}\boldsymbol{\beta} $$ (13.28b)

formulas.label: Formula 13.28c
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: The additive variance in fitness that remains unaccounted for after the effects of z are removed becomes $$ \sigma_{A}^{2}(w)-\beta^{T}\mathbf{G}\beta $$ (13.28c)

formulas.label: Formula 13.29
formulas.subsection: Selection Gradients and Mean Population Fitness
formulas.context_text: ter 6). If the fitness determined by the vector of traits, z, can be expressed as a linear regression (Equation 13.25b), then the expected change in fitness from selection response on these traits is $$ \Delta\overline{w}=\beta^{T}\Delta\mathbf{z}=\beta^{T}\mathbf{R}=\beta^{T}\mathbf{G}\beta $$ (13.29) From Equation 13.28b, this is just the additive variance in fitness associated with these traits. Thus, we recover Fisher's theorem that the change in mean fitness equals the additive variance in rel
